coredata.dc:title: Histidine-functionalized silver nanoclusters used as a blue-emissive fluorescence probe for vitamin B12 detection
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Shen
Wang, Xiao
Wang, Yutong
Dong, Jiaxu
Zhang, Yixuan
Zhao, Manlin
coredata.dc:description: Vitamin B12 is a drug that can participate in the production of bone marrow red blood cells, prevention of pernicious anemia and damage to brain nerves. It is needful to prepare new vitamin B12 determination strategies with excellent sensitivity, selectivity and uniqueness. Herein, a novel and facile chemical reduction method was successfully carried out (histidine and ascorbic acid were applied as protectant and reductant) for preparing histidine-functionalized silver nanoclusters (Ag NCs@his) with blue fluorescence. Some technologies were adopted to clarify the structure, optical characteristics of this stable Ag NCs@his and fluorescence quenching mechanism. Based on inner filter effect, a sensitive and selective platform for vitamin B12 detection was established. Under optimum pH value, reaction time and temperature, the as-developed platform could be utilized to the trace measurement of vitamin B12 with linear range of 0.5–200 μM and detection limit of 0.038 μM. Moreover, this platform was successfully used to measure vitamin B12 in real samples and temperature.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silver nanoclusters
Histidine
Fluorescence
Vitamin B12
Chemical probes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Vitamin B12 sensing in real samples
Characterization of the as-developed Ag NCs@his
Optimization of vitamin B12 detection conditions
Sensing of vitamin B12 in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We would like to thank the College Students’ Innovation Program of Taiyuan Normal University (No. CXCY2221 ) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Excitation and emission spectra of Ag NCs@his (A); Effects of excitation wavelength on emission spectra (B); FT-IR spectra of histidine and Ag NCs@his (C).
TEM image of Ag NCs@his (A), XPS of Ag NCs@his (B), C1s (C), N1s (D), O1s (E) and Ag3d (F).
Emission spectra of Ag NCs@his with different amounts of vitamin B12 (A); The fitted linear curve of ln(F0/F) and vitamin B12 concentrations (B).
UV–vis spectrum of vitamin B12, excitation and emission spectra of Ag NCs@his (A); Fluorescence lifetime of Ag NCs@his with and without vitamin B12 (B).
Emission spectra of Ag NCs@his at different temperature (A); Linear curve of FL intensity and temperature (B); Cycle curve of FL intensity between 25 and 95 ℃ (C).
Comparison of different analytical methods for vitamin B12 determination.
Vitamin B12 detection in real samples.

head.ce:title: Histidine-functionalized silver nanoclusters used as a blue-emissive fluorescence probe for vitamin B12 detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong 030619, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong 030619, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Vitamin B12 is a drug that can participate in the production of bone marrow red blood cells, prevention of pernicious anemia and damage to brain nerves. It is needful to prepare new vitamin B12 determination strategies with excellent sensitivity, selectivity and uniqueness. Herein, a novel and facile chemical reduction method was successfully carried out (histidine and ascorbic acid were applied as protectant and reductant) for preparing histidine-functionalized silver nanoclusters (Ag NCs@his) with blue fluorescence. Some technologies were adopted to clarify the structure, optical characteristics of this stable Ag NCs@his and fluorescence quenching mechanism. Based on inner filter effect, a sensitive and selective platform for vitamin B12 detection was established. Under optimum pH value, reaction time and temperature, the as-developed platform could be utilized to the trace measurement of vitamin B12 with linear range of 0.5–200 μM and detection limit of 0.038 μM. Moreover, this platform was successfully used to measure vitamin B12 in real samples and temperature.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, metal nanoclusters (MNCs) have drawn noticeable interest of researchers because of their interesting characteristics vary from organic dyes, metal organic skeleton structure and carbon quantum dots, such as lower biotoxicity, good water solubility, strong biocompatibility, significant fluorescence intensity and excellent stability [1–4]. Thus, different strategies were carried out to develop MNCs, such as template method, chemical reduction method, etching method, electrochemical method and microwave assisted method [5–8]. In MNCs, gold nanoclusters (Au NCs), silver nanoclusters (Ag NCs) and copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) were widely synthesized and used. Compared with gold and copper, the significant price advantage than Au NCs and more difficult to be oxidized than Cu NCs made Ag NCs gradually attract more attentions in many areas, such as sensors, photocatalysis, biomarkers and bioimaging [9–12].

However, the development of Ag NCs was a significant challenge due to the difficulty in preparing excellently stable nanoparticles. To address the above issues, some researchers have tried many methods for the preparation of stabilized Ag NCs. To our knowledge, some substances (containing active functional groups) have been used as protective agents, such as polyethyleneimine, dihydrolipoic acid, tryptophan, bovine serum albumin and so on [13–16]. Ag+ was restored to Ag0 using some reducing agents, such as sodium borohydride, hydrazine hydrate and vitamin C [17,18]. Both of the active functional groups and reducibility made histidine to be a better choice for synthesizing Ag NCs. Furthermore, the Ag NCs have been used to detection ochratoxin A, biothiols, berberine hydrochloride, thiram and aloin [19–22].

Vitamin B12, a polycyclic compound containing 3-valent cobalt, is the only vitamin containing metallic elements and mainly present in animal foods such as meat, fish, dairy products and eggs [23,24]. It is involved in producing bone marrow red blood cells, preventing pernicious anemia and damage to brain nerves. Vitamin B12 is also fundamental towards the biological preparation of substances such as DNA. Thus, it is very necessary to master the specific concentration of vitamin B12 [25,26]. To today, some techniques have been employed to determine vitamin B12, such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [27,28], electrochemical methods [29,30] and fluorescence methods [31,32]. As is known to all, fluorescence methods held some advantages in comparison with other methods, such as easy operation, high sensitivity and fast response speed. And the key factors of this method was proper probe with excellent properties.

Herein, in this paper, a facile and efficient strategy was successfully utilized to develop silver nanoclusters protected by histidine with strong blue fluorescence. Amusingly, the as-developed histidine-functionalized silver nanoclusters (Ag NCs@his) displayed apparent water-solubility and stability. When detected vitamin B12 amounts, the blue fluorescence of Ag NCs@his was quenched. The linear range and detection limit of Ag NCs@his for vitamin B12 were studied. The sensitivity and selectivity of this fluorescence strategy for vitamin B12 were also investigated. Furthermore, the feasibility for vitamin B12 detection was discussed in real samples.

Silver nitrate (99.8 %), histidine (≥99 %), ascorbic acid (≥99 %), sodium chloride (99.5 %), potassium chloride (99.5 %), sodium carbonate (≥99.5 %), sodium bicarbonate (≥99.8 %), sodium acetate (≥99 %), glucose (98 %), glutathione (98 %), tyramine (98 %), metronidazole (99 %), histamine (96 %), artemisinin (98 %), vitamin B6 (≥98 %), vitamin B1 (97 %), vitamin B12 (98 %), tyrosine (99 %), tryptophan (99 %), glycine (99 %), alanine (99 %), phenylalanine (99 %), chloramphenicol (98 %), streptomycin (≥95 %) and paracetamol (98 %) was purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Shanghai, China. Na2HPO4-NaH2PO4 buffer solutions (pH 6.0–8.0) were self-preparation. Ultrapure water was obtained through ultrapure water instrument and used for preparing different solutions.

All fluorescence spectra were obtained on an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and excitation and emission slits were set as 10 nm and 10 nm, respectively. Meanwhile, a four sided transparent cuvette was used for fluorescence measurement. UV–vis absorption spectra and absorbance were acquired using a UV-4500 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) with wavelength range of 200–800 nm. The mixing and reaction of samples were carried out through thermostatic magnetic stirrer (DF-101, Gongyi, China) at different temperature. The particle size and morphology of Ag NCs@his were obtained through transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images, which were carried out on a Hitachi-7700 TEM (Hitachi, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nexus 8700, USA) was applied to discuss the surface groups of Ag NCs@his with wavenumber range of 4000–400 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250, USA) was used to analyze valence state of elements in Ag NCs@his. The fluorescence lifetime of Ag NCs@his with and without vitamin B12 were obtained on an FLS1000 (Edinburgh, England).

Herein, 0.005 M AgNO3 solution of 500 μL and 0.05 M ascorbic acid solution of 200 μL were slowly dropped into 0.05 M histidine solution of 5 mL and 10 mL H2O at constant temperature followed by stir for 5 min. The ultimate pH value of solution was transferred to 5. Then the mixture was placed into thermostat water bath and reacted at 55 ℃ for 5 h. Afterwards, the obtained yellow solution was centrifuged at 1000r for 15 min to remove suspended solids, and the solution was dialyzed by dialysis bag (MW: 1000 Da) for 12 h. The as-developed Ag NCs@his solution was stored in the refrigerator (4 °C) for further use.

For fluorescence analysis, different vitamin B12 concentration (10 μL) and detection system (0.5 mL Ag NCs@his and 1.5 mL buffer solution) were mixed sufficiently at 25 ℃ for 40 s. Then, the fluorescence emission spectra and intensity of Ag NCs@his with different vitamin B12 concentration were recorded. The linear range and detection limit between ln(F0/F) and vitamin B12 concentration were obtained by processing the above data (F0 and F refer to the fluorescence emission intensity of detection system without and with vitamin B12, respectively).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Vitamin B12 sensing in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Bovine serum samples were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Shanghai, China. Mengniu milk was obtained from supermarket. The obtained samples were diluted 10 times with buffer solution (pH = 6.0). Then, different vitamin B12 concentrations were spiked into the above samples. The linear fitting equation was applied to calculate recovery rate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the as-developed Ag NCs@his
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation conditions of Ag NCs@his (such as histidine volume, ascorbic acid volume, pH, reaction temperature and reaction time) were discussed. As displayed in Fig. S1, the optimal histidine volume, ascorbic acid volume, pH, reaction temperature and reaction time were 5 mL, 200 μL, 5, 55 ℃ and 5 h, respectively.

The optical features of Ag NCs@his were investigated through UV–vis, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra. As suggested in Fig. 1 A, the maximum emission wavelength displayed around 466 nm when the excitation wavelength was set as 381 nm. Additionally, we would confirm whether emission wavelengths were changed by variation of excitation wavelength. In Fig. 1B, when the excitation wavelength changed from 360 nm to 400 nm, the emission wavelength did not have a fixed value, suggesting Ag NCs@his’s excitation-dependent feature [33,34]. The most widely adopted explanation of excitation-dependent behavior was quantum confinement, surface defect or size effect. To study surface groups of Ag NCs@his, FT-IR was carried out through squash method. As performed in Fig. 1C, some functional groups could be observed, such as hydroxyl, amino, carbonyl and carbon–carbon double bond. Peaks at 3411.5, 2975.3 and 2883.2 cm−1 were assigned to stretching vibration of O-H and C-H bonds [35]. The stretching vibration peaks of C = O, C = C, C-N and C-O bonds were located at 1637.9, 1595.6, 1348.9 and 1255.4 cm−1, respectively [36]. Peaks at 1463.5 and 1152.1 cm−1 were assumed to deformation vibration peaks of C-H and O-H bonds, respectively [37]. These functional groups clearly belong to histidine and also illustrated the successful introduction of histidine to Ag NCs@his.

Additionally, the TEM image was displayed in Fig. 2 A and Ag NCs@his presented a uniform spherical shape. The average particle size was 2.6 ± 0.2 nm by counting 100 particles. Moreover, XPS was used as an analysis method to discuss elemental composition. In XPS full spectrum of Ag NCs@his (Fig. 2B), binding energy of C, N, O and Ag were located at 284.2 eV, 400.4 eV, 531.5 eV and 372.1 eV, respectively [38]. In XPS single spectrum of C1s (Fig. 2C), three peaks at 284.1 eV, 285.4 eV and 287.6 eV resulted from peak division of C1s, which were assigned to C-C, C-N and O-C = O bonds [39,40]. In XPS single spectrum of N1s (Fig. 2D), feature peaks of C-N and N-H bonds were located at 398.5 eV and 400.6 eV [41,42]. In Fig. 2E, O1s single spectrum possessed three peaks at 530.7 eV, 531.6 eV and 532.5 eV, which were assumed to –OH, C = O and C-O bonds [43]. The above structures corresponded well with FT-IR results. From Fig. 2F, Ag3d XPS spectrum had two peaks at 367.9 eV and 373.9 eV, which were attributed to Ag0 [44,45]. At the same time, we could obtain the content of each element based on results of XPS that C of 58.75 %, N of 15.47 %, O of 24.56 % and Ag of 1.22 %.

To our knowledge, the stability of probe was crucial for future widespread applications in different environments. We studied the stability of Ag NCs@his under different conditions, such as storage time, UV radiation time, NaCl solution and pH. Paraphrasing, from Fig. S2A, we first studied effects of storage time on the stability under 5 d, 10 d, 15 d, 20 d and 25 d. A fascinating phenomenon was observed that the fluorescence emission intensity of Ag NCs@his did not attenuate after storage of 25 d at room temperature. Additionally, ultraviolet radiation also affects the stability of probe. After UV irradiation for 5 min, the fluorescence intensity had only 4.5 % quenching degree, suggesting fascinating UV resistance for Ag NCs@his (Fig. S2B). To study the salt resistance of Ag NCs@his, different concentrations of NaCl were added into Ag NCs@his solution, and fluorescence intensities were recorded through molecular fluorescence analyzer. Although the concentration of sodium chloride reached 0.05 M, fluorescence intensity of Ag NCs@his solution did not appear weakening phenomenon and an enhanced trend was observed (Fig. S2C). Moreover, the influence of pH on fluorescence stability was discussed. As displayed in Fig. S2D, when the pH value increased from 4 to 6, fluorescence intensity displayed an increasing trend. Then the fluorescence intensity decreased slowly as pH increased and reached maximum value at pH of 6. Therefore, the subsequent testing environment was basically maintained at pH of around 6.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of vitamin B12 detection conditions
ce:section.ce:para: To effectuate the maximum fluorescence quenching of Ag NCs@his for vitamin B12 determination, some detection parameters were discussed, such as pH value of buffer solution, detection temperature and incubation time. The quenching rate ((F0-F)/F0, F0 and F stood for fluorescence intensity of probe in absence and presence of vitamin B12) was calculated to evaluate detection effect. In Fig. S3A, influences of buffer solution pH (6.0–8.0) on detection performance was first studied. From Fig. S3A, we found that the quenching efficiency reached maximum value at pH value of 6. Then, fluorescence intensities were obtained without and with vitamin B12 under different temperatures. In Fig. S3B, the quenching rate gradually decreased with increasing temperature from 25 ℃ to 65 ℃. Apparently, the optimal performance of this detection platform reached at 25 ℃. Furthermore, the fluorescence quenching degree quickly reached the maximum (Fig. S3C) at 40 s. The subsequent extension of reaction time did not result in significant changes in quenching efficiency.

As we can see from Fig. 3 A, under the optimum pH value of buffer solution, detection temperature and incubation time (6, 25 ℃ and 40 s), distinct amounts of vitamin B12 (0–200 μM) were dropped into determination platform. The ln(F0/F) value was rectilinear to amounts of vitamin B12 over the range of 0.5–200 μM (Fig. 3B). The fitted linear equation was ln(F0/F) = 0.01[Q] + 0.08 with fitting coefficient of 0.9910 and detection limit (LOD) of 0.038 μM (Q refers to amounts of vitamin B12). Comparison of different methods for vitamin B12 sensing was displayed in Table 1 . As can be seen from Table 1, this method suggested significant linear range and LOD.

To study whether this developed Ag NCs@his was selective for vitamin B12 sensing, some substances (200 μM of Na+, K+, CO3 2–, HCO3 –, CH3COO–, glucose, glutathione, tyramine, metronidazole, histamine, artemisinin, vitamin B6, vitamin B1, tyrosine, tryptophan, glycine, alanine, phenylalanine, chloramphenicol, streptomycin and paracetamol) were used to discuss selectivity under the optimum conditions. From Fig. S4A, Ag NCs@his possessed significant selectivity for vitamin B12 over these control substances. At the same time, the control substances had weak influence on fluorescence intensity of Ag NCs@his. When vitamin B12 was added into Ag NCs@his containing control substances, the fluorescence could also be prominently quenched, suggesting a fascinating selectivity of this nanosensor for determination of vitamin B12 (Fig. S4B).

In Fig. 4 A (UV–vis absorption spectrum of vitamin B12), two significant absorption peak at 358 and 559 nm were obtained. Significantly, the UV absorption spectrum of vitamin B12 overlapped with excitation and emission spectra of Ag NCs@his, suggesting the possibility of inner filter effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [46,47]. The hypothesis was further confirmed through fluorescence lifetime. As displayed in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence lifetime of Ag NCs@his without and with vitamin B12 were 5.14 ns and 5.08 ns, respectively. The difference of fluorescence lifetime could be almost ignored. So the FRET was excluded and inner filter effect should be the main quenching mechanism [48,49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of vitamin B12 in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To measure the amounts of vitamin B12 in bovine serum and mengniu milk samples, 500 μL of Ag NCs@his solution was diluted with 1.5 mL PBS solution (pH = 6), and the mixture was reacted at 25 ℃ for 40 s. The weakness of fluorescence intensities was not observed, suggesting that vitamin B12 was not determined in sensing system. For estimating accuracy of this as-established novel platform, the utilization of Ag NCs@his-based nanoprobe for vitamin B12 detection in bovine serum and mengniu milk samples were further discussed through recovery testing. Various amounts of normalized vitamin B12 solution were added into bovine serum and mengniu milk samples. Afterwards, the spiked samples were dropped into determination solution. After reacted at 25 ℃ for 5 min, the corresponding fluorescence data were obtained and used to calculate recoveries based on linear equation. In Table 2 , the recovery rates in bovine serum and mengniu milk samples were 95.7–95.8 % and 97.7–98.0 %, respectively. All relative standard deviation (RSD) were lower than 3.49 %. The testing results indicated that this as-prepared Ag NCs@his-based fluorescence nanoprobe could be applied for vitamin B12 testing in real samples.

As we can see from Fig. 5 A and B, FL intensity decreased with increasing temperature (25–85 ℃) and was rectilinear to temperature over the range of 25–85 ℃ (FL represents the fluorescence intensity). The fitted linear equation was F = -12.6 T + 1895.4 with fitting coefficient of 0.9711 (T refers to detection temperature). Furthermore, when the temperature dropped again to 25 ℃, the fluorescence intensity of sensing solution could restore to initial value. Meanwhile, the sensing solution could cycle multiple times between 25 ℃ and 95 ℃ without significant decrease of fluorescence intensity. Thus, this developed platform is promising as a sensor for temperature sensing.

Herein, we put forward a facile strategy for preparation of Ag NCs@his with blue emitting fluorescence. In this development procedure, histidine was applied as a capping reagent and ascorbic acid was used as a reductant. This stable Ag NCs@his was applied for selective and sensitive determination of vitamin B12. Under the optimum pH value of buffer solution, detection temperature and incubation time, the linear relationship of ln(F0/F) and vitamin B12 concentration (ranging from 0.5 to 200 μM) was established. Finally, the excellent performance of this nanoprobe for detecting vitamin B12 in real samples and temperature stated that this developed platform is promising for sensitive and selective measurement of vitamin B12.
Shen Zhang: Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Xiao Wang: . Yutong Wang: Software. Jiaxu Dong: Software. Yixuan Zhang: Software. Manlin Zhao: Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank the College Students’ Innovation Program of Taiyuan Normal University (No. CXCY2221) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.109985.
